title: Walking in Wisdom
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Walking in Wisdom - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 5_15-21.txt
outline: See then that you walk circumspectly, not as fools but as wise
Redeeming the time, for the days are evil
Understanding what the will of the Lord is
Do not be drunk with wine, but be filled with the Spirit
Speaking in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs
Conclusion: The Spirit-filled life as the mark of wisdom
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Ephesians 5:15-21 on walking in wisdom. Paul calls believers to walk carefully and wisely, redeeming the time in evil days, understanding God's will, and being filled with the Spirit — expressed in corporate worship through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Christian Walk

Paul continues in this passage on the theme of the Christian life as a walk. In Ephesians 5:1–7, the Christian is called to walk in love toward one another. In verses 8–14, the Christian is called to walk as a child of light toward the world. And now in verses 15–21, we see that the Christian is called to walk in wisdom — in relation to one another and to the world.

In our passage, the New King James Version says in verse 15 that we are to walk "circumspectly" — a word we don't use often in common language, which simply means to walk carefully. Paul means by that that we are to walk in wisdom. The Christian is to walk thoughtfully, carefully, and purposefully. The Christian life is not a wandering around the earth — it is a purposeful, careful walk toward the end that God calls us to fulfill.

## The Structure of the Passage

The expression "walk circumspectly" is the controlling thought throughout this passage, all the way from verse 15 to verse 21. Everything Paul describes here is how a Christian walks carefully, how a Christian walks in wisdom. This walk is described by several participles and verbs.

In verse 16, we are told that this wise walk is done by **redeeming the time**. In verse 17, Paul says this walk in wisdom is done by not being unwise — he likes a double negative, and Paul is one of my heroes for that reason, much as Peter is for blurting things out when the opportunity is there. He says, "Let me tell you how you walk in wisdom. You walk in wisdom by not walking unwisely." Very straightforward.

But he also says in verse 17 a third way to walk in wisdom: by **understanding the will of the Lord**. Then in verse 18, we walk in wisdom by not being drunk. The fifth way we walk in wisdom is by being **filled with the Spirit** — and notice that is a command. Being filled is not something that happens by accident; it is something we are purposely to pursue.

Verses 19–21 describe how one is filled with the Spirit. All those participles — "speaking," "singing," "making melody," "giving thanks," "submitting" — describe the way you are filled with the Spirit. Some translations (ESV, NIV) place verse 21 with the passage that follows, but I believe grammatically it belongs here as an element of being filled with the Spirit: a willingness to submit to one another.

Paul also always uses pairs to contrast and compare. In verses 1–7 it is love versus licentiousness. In verses 8–14 it is light versus darkness. And now in verses 15–21 he compares **wisdom with folly**.

Also notice the connecting word "therefore" (or "then") in verse 15: *because* these things are true of us — we've been brought out of darkness, we are light, we walk in love — *therefore* we walk circumspectly, we walk in wisdom.

## What Is Wisdom?

Before we proceed, we need to understand what wisdom is. Wisdom is simply **applying what you know from Scripture to daily life**. Wisdom is taking what you've read in the Bible and living it out — taking the principles of Scripture and applying them in everyday life. That's it, simply that. And that is a very important characteristic of the Christian life.

## First Characteristic: Redeeming the Time (Ephesians 5:15–16)

> *"See then that you walk circumspectly, not as fools but as wise, redeeming the time, because the days are evil."* (Ephesians 5:15–16)

What I'm going to say here may be contrary to what you've heard before about this passage. When Paul urges us to redeem the time, this is not so much an exhortation to be productive or efficient as it is an exhortation to **take advantage of the opportunities that God brings to you** — not simply "live every minute to the fullest."

The New Testament uses two words for time. One is *chronos*, which refers generally to the flow of time — minutes, days, hours, months, years. The English word "chronology" comes from *chronos*. The other word is *kairos*, which refers to a moment that is significant or favorable — an opportunity, a season. Not so much *when* something happens, but that a particular opportunity is present. We've all used the expression "Remember that time" — we're not talking about 3:55 p.m., we're talking about a particular opportunity or event. *Kairos* is the word Paul uses here in verse 16. Paul is not talking about using every minute to the best of your ability; he's talking about **the opportunities, the events that God brings into your life**.

Charles Hodge renders the first half of verse 16 as "availing yourselves of the occasion" — avail yourself of the occasions that God has brought into your life, improving every opportunity for good. The ESV expresses this well: "making the best use of the time."

Now Paul is not explicit here in Ephesians 5 about what opportunities he has in mind, but in Colossians 4:5–6 he is very explicit. Keep your finger in Ephesians 5 and turn there for a moment:

> *"Walk in wisdom toward those who are outside, redeeming the time. Let your speech always be with grace, seasoned with salt, that you may know how you ought to answer each one."* (Colossians 4:5–6)

The same language — walking in wisdom, redeeming the time — but here Paul adds: walking in wisdom **toward those who are outside**. And our speech is to be with grace, seasoned with salt, so that we may answer each one.

Putting these two passages together, I conclude that what Paul is talking about is redeeming our time in the area of **evangelism**. We are to make the most of the opportunities God gives us to shine the light of Jesus Christ into the darkness of this world, so that people can come to know Christ and be transformed by Him.

### Our Speech Must Be Gracious

You've heard the saying attributed to Francis of Assisi: "Preach the gospel at all times — when necessary, use words." That sounds so pious, so humble. It is as foolish a statement as can be made. You cannot proclaim the gospel without using words. That is the excuse of someone too afraid to share the gospel with another person. People are not going to come to know Jesus simply by watching you.

The gospel is a series of propositions — statements of fact — that must be communicated by the use of language. When Paul says to the Corinthians, "I delivered to you what is of first importance," he doesn't list a series of things he *did* for them to observe. He lists a series of things he *told* them:

> *"That Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, and that He was buried, and that He rose again the third day according to the Scriptures, and that He was seen by Cephas, then by the twelve."* (1 Corinthians 15:3–5)

You might say, "But I don't know how to speak the gospel to people." If you don't know what the gospel is, then you are not a Christian. The gospel is this: Jesus Christ, the perfect Son of God, took flesh and became human like you and me. He obeyed God perfectly — the thing you and I cannot do. Having completed His perfect life, He died the death a sinner deserves — the death you and I deserved. The Father accepted that sacrifice by raising Him from the dead, and now He sits at the Father's right hand. If you don't believe that Jesus did that for you, you are condemned. But today is the day of salvation.

### Grace-Seasoned Speech

Colossians 4 says our speech is to be **always with grace, seasoned with salt**. There is no exemption from this — not a political exemption, not a philosophical exemption. We are not to destroy people with ungracious words. All who are outside are the objects of our evangelism. There is no one outside of Jesus Christ that we should not want to come to faith in Him.

We cannot discredit the gospel by the way we speak to or about unbelievers. Our speech must be wholesome and attractive — "seasoned with salt." Salt was used for preservation and for taste. Our words must be well thought out, understandable, wholesome, and attractive. We are not trying to make the gospel *more* attractive — the gospel is the story of the beautiful Savior — but we do present the Savior in all His attractiveness. We do not try to make the gospel more palatable to our culture by adding the wrong seasoning. We let Christ shine as we present the gospel.

### The Unbeliever's Questions

Look again at Colossians 4:6: "that you may know how you ought to answer each one." Paul's assumption is that unbelievers will raise questions about the faith. They will look at you and wonder: *Who are these people? Why is it that they act and speak so differently? Why can they rejoice in the midst of severe persecution?* That is the natural result of truly redeeming the time.

Is that the effect you are having in the world? Do people ask you why you're so different? Remember what Peter says: everyone should be ready to give a reason for the hope that lies within them, with meekness and fear (1 Peter 3:15). Your obedience to Christ, demonstrated in attitude and actions and speech, causes people to wonder about you.

Teenagers, younger people — it is not enough to assume your parents' faith. You must believe for yourself. Can you formulate your own confession of faith? Are you able to say, "I know whom I have believed, and I am persuaded that He is able to keep what I have committed to Him against that day" (2 Timothy 1:12)?

This faith is not just for your parents. It is for you. God has been merciful in placing you in a church and family that believes in Jesus Christ. Do not squander that mercy.

## The Urgency of the Days

What is the motivation Paul gives us to redeem the time? Look at verse 16 again: "Redeeming the time, because **the days are evil**." The days we live in are a reflection of the reality of the fall. They are wicked. They are immoral. And because of that, they are moving toward judgment. Every day we are moving closer to the judgment of God. We do not know how much time is left. And by saying this, Paul adds a sense of urgency.

The unbeliever — and even we ourselves — may not live to see tomorrow. We don't know how much time is left. And every moment is moving toward judgment. We must take advantage of every opportunity. We must redeem the time by considering the unbelievers in our lives. We all have unbelievers in our lives — children, parents, siblings, coworkers, schoolmates. What are the opportunities God has given us to speak grace into their lives? Are we redeeming the time?

We often say, "But God hasn't opened a door." Usually what that means is that God slammed the door open and we ran after it, grabbed the handle, and shut it closed. The world is dying. We have a reigning King who has appointed us to bring the gospel of salvation to the nations — and that mission starts with the unbeliever next to us. Is that person going to go to hell because we thought it was too inconvenient, too shameful, too awkward to speak the gospel to them?

I am a Calvinist. I am Reformed. I believe in the sovereignty of God. And I believe that the sovereign God uses you and me to persuade that person. As a believer, you must stand between the unbeliever and hell, doing whatever you can to say, "Do not fall in."

## Second Characteristic: Understanding the Will of God (Ephesians 5:17)

> *"Therefore do not be unwise, but understanding what the will of the Lord is."* (Ephesians 5:17)

We sometimes make the will of God unnecessarily complicated. We think, "I need some mystical, abstract experience to tell me exactly what God's will is for me." We tend to think of God's will as a bull's-eye, as if we might be at the outer rings and only get ten points instead of one hundred. That's not how it works.

God's will is more like a large park, whose borders are the Word of God. Anywhere you are inside that park, you are in the will of God. Whether to marry Christian A or Christian B — both are in the will of God (not at the same time, of course). You don't need to wait for a bird to land on a tree as a sign. God's will is simply to know the principles of His Word and apply them to the unique situations of your own life.

In the context of Ephesians 5, this is primarily related to making the most of the opportunities God has given us to share the gospel with unbelievers. I will tell you what the will of God is for you right now: **share your faith with a dying world**. You don't need a magic eight ball, a crystal ball, a fleece, or casting lots. The will of God for every person who claims to follow Jesus Christ is to share the gospel.

We are called to be wise — exposing and rebuking the darkness of evil behavior and false ideas, while at the same time persuading people to believe in Jesus with speech seasoned with grace. We are to expose darkness not in the manner of those who would destroy people in the process. One purpose of exposing darkness is so that people may come out of it and into the kingdom of God's dear Son.

## Conclusion

Are you going to walk in wisdom? Are you going to make the most of the opportunities God has given you to proclaim the gospel to a dying world — starting with the unbelievers already in your life? That is the will of God for you. That is what Paul says in God's Word.
